metadata.sources: EnergySage 2026 marketplace data, NREL ATB 2026, SEIA Solar Market Insight, EIA, contractor surveys, BBB solar complaint volume tracking

perWattInstalled.notes: EnergySage 2026 national average ~$2.85/W gross before incentives. Per-watt comes down at larger system sizes.

panelTiers.label: Tier 1 — premium
panelTiers.notes: 25-year product warranties, 92%+ output retention at year 25, lowest annual degradation.

panelTiers.label: Tier 1 — value
panelTiers.notes: Most installers' default 2026 choice. 25-year linear warranties, good cost-per-watt.

panelTiers.label: Tier 2
panelTiers.notes: Lower-volume manufacturers with shorter warranties and weaker financial backing. Bankability risk if the manufacturer fails before warranty period ends.

inverterTiers.label: Microinverter
inverterTiers.notes: Per-panel optimization. Best for shaded or complex roof orientations. 25-year warranty standard. Slight production premium vs string.

inverterTiers.label: String inverter with DC optimizers
inverterTiers.notes: Per-panel optimization with central inverter. Lower upfront cost than microinverters. SolarEdge financial position is a known watch item — ask for inverter warranty backing.

inverterTiers.label: Pure string inverter
inverterTiers.notes: Cheapest option. Single shading event reduces output of the entire string. Best for unshaded southern roofs only.

system_4kw.label: 4 kW system
system_4kw.notes: Small home, good for $80-$120/mo electric bills.

iraCredit.section: IRA Section 25D Residential Clean Energy Credit
iraCredit.notes: 30% federal credit with no cap on residential solar PV, solar water heating, battery storage 3kWh+, geothermal, fuel cells. Active through 2032 under current statute. NOT affected by the 25C expiration that hit windows, HVAC, insulation Dec 31 2025 — 25D is a separate IRA section. Includes equipment, labor, permits, and interconnection costs in the credit basis. No income limits. Principal residence only. Claim on IRS Form 5695 Part I.

stateNetMetering.notes: Net metering rules drive solar payback as much as system price. NEM 3.0 in California cut export rates ~75% in 2023; many states followed.
stateNetMetering.warning: Always check the SPECIFIC utility, not just the state. State-level NEM rules don't apply uniformly across all utilities.

utilityRebates.notes: ConnectedSolutions battery program. Pays per discharge event.
SREC market — varies; check Illinois Shines portal.

redFlagPatterns.regex: \b(ppa|power purchase agreement|lease|leased|escalator|monthly payment for 25 years|$0 down)\b
redFlagPatterns.explanation: Quote is a PPA or lease, not a purchase. Homeowner doesn't own the panels, doesn't get the 30% federal tax credit (the leasing company does), and the contract often includes a 2-3% annual escalator. Total lifetime cost is often 2-3x cash purchase. PPAs and leases also create lien/title complications when selling the home.

System sized larger than 110% of historical annual usage. Most utilities won't credit excess production. Common high-commission scam: oversize the system, finance the difference, lock the homeowner into a 25-year payment for power they can't use.

Solar loan with payment exceeding the projected utility bill savings. Common pattern: low advertised rate + dealer fee buried in price (15-25% markup). Always compare cash price quote vs financed price quote — the gap is the dealer fee.

redFlagPatterns.regex: \b(production estimate|annual production|guaranteed production|kwh per year|year 1 production)\b
redFlagPatterns.explanation: Production estimate not derived from a documented PVWatts or shading analysis. Inflating year-1 production by 15-25% is the most common misrepresentation in solar sales. Always demand a PVWatts simulation or HelioScope shading report.
Solar panel quote with no brand or model named. Tier 1 panels cost 50-100% more than Tier 2 — without a brand you cannot benchmark price-fairness or warranty backing.
Inverter not specified. Inverter is the most failure-prone component. Microinverters (Enphase) vs string vs string-with-optimizer dramatically affects production on shaded roofs.

No mention of warranties. Solar has 4 separate warranties: panel product, panel performance, inverter, workmanship/labor. Reputable installers offer 25-year panel/performance, 12-25 year inverter, and 10+ year workmanship. Anything less is below market.
No mention of permits or utility interconnection. Both are required. Without PTO (Permission to Operate) the system is illegal to energize and ineligible for net metering credits.

Solar quote with no roof condition assessment. Reputable installers refuse to install on roofs with less than 10 years of remaining life. Mounting solar on a failing roof means re-doing it (and the panels) within 5-10 years.
Deposit over 25% on solar is high risk. Many state contractor laws cap solar deposits at 10% (CA: $1,000 or 10%). Reputable installers stage payments at install milestones.
No critter guard mentioned. Squirrels and birds nest under arrays and chew wiring, causing arc faults. $500-$1,500 added install but worth it on most homes.
Quote doesn't reference your historical electricity consumption. Sizing solar without knowing the home's actual usage is malpractice.
Promises to 'eliminate your electric bill' or '100% offset' are usually false. Most homes still pay non-bypassable charges, demand fees, and connection charges even with a zero-net-energy system.
Solar loan or lease that places a UCC-1 or fixture filing on the home. This complicates refinancing and home sale. Always read the fine print and require lien releases.

Sales rep claims the federal solar credit has been removed. False. The 25D Residential Clean Energy Credit is active at 30% through 2032 under current statute. The 25C credit (windows, HVAC, insulation) expired Dec 31 2025 but that is a different IRA section. Don't let a sales rep confuse them.

scopeCheckItems.label: Inverter brand, model, and topology named
PTO timeline and net metering enrollment

stateLicensing.notes: Solar installers typically need a state electrical contractor license OR a state solar contractor license. A general contractor license is usually NOT sufficient.

questionsToAskContractor: Is this quote a cash purchase, loan, lease, or PPA?
What panel brand and model are you proposing? Show me the spec sheet.
What inverter — micro, string, or string with optimizers?
What is the year-1 production estimate and what tool generated it (PVWatts, HelioScope)?
What is my historical kWh usage and what offset percentage does this system provide?
What is the roof condition? Do I need a re-roof first?
What are the panel, inverter, and workmanship warranties separately?
What permits are needed and who pulls them?
What is the timeline to PTO and what is your interconnection track record with my utility?
What is the deposit and payment milestone schedule?
Are there any UCC-1 or fixture filings tied to the financing?
How will the federal 25D credit be reflected (purchase) or assigned (PPA/lease)?
